LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Calculated as the closure of a Land Surface System water budget for the Chowchilla Subbasin (closure of each water use sector was calculated separately for each subregion). All other Land Surface System water budget components were quantified using measured volumes (where available) or using unitized IWFM Demand Calculator (IDC) model results multiplied by land use areas. Land use data in 2019 came from County Agriculture Commission land use data (spatially interpolated from DWR Land Use Survey results and Land IQ coverages using the DWR interpolation tool).
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Typical uncertainty when calculated for Land Surface System water balance closure. Uncertainty of the water budget closure is calculated as the combined uncertainty of all other water budget flow paths following the procedure described by Clemmens and Burt (1997), as discussed in the Annual Report. The uncertainties of all other flow paths are quantified based on measurement device accuracy, technical literature, or professional judgment.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Meters and comparable measuring devices operated by local water agencies, water purveyors and city water departments in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: These data were collected from well meters by experienced water agency staff following monitoring protocols for urban and agricultural water supply.  Meters are considered to be well-maintained and tested; therefore, reported data meet high accuracy levels in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards. In accordance with these standards for water supply, meter accuracy is assumed to range between 0% and 5%.
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: IWFM Demand Calculator (IDC) in C2VSimFG-Kern
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated groundwater extractions is based on simulation results of the IDC tool within C2VSimFG-Kern model.  The water balance accuracy of the groundwater model is considered to be medium, approximately ranging between 10% and 20%.  Input data based on metered data is assumed to have an accuracy of 0% to 5%.  The agricultural pumping, which is the largest component of groundwater extraction, are estimated using the ITRC-METRIC based ET Rates.  ITRC (2020) lists the accuracy of the ET measurements as ranging from 7% to 10%. Land use is based on Kern County Agricultural Commissioner annual land use data including crop type.  Soil properties are assumed are based on local soil survey data which have a higher level of uncertainty.  Applying a weighted average of these inputs to the overall water budget produces a relative accuracy for the land use calculation for agricultural groundwater extractions ranging between 10% and 20%.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extraction data from groundwater agencies located in the Merced Subbasin were directly measured. Data are a mixture of metered and some data from pump tests using run time data.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Metered data have a qualitative high level of accuracy. Many agencies reported metered groundwater pumping data and did not have information on an exact percent accuracy.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extractions from private irrigators and domestic wells are estimated by the Merced Water Resources Model (MercedWRM) based on factors including land use, evapotranspiration, and population. Details about the MercedWRM can be found in the GSP and Section 3.5.3 of the Annual Report.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The estimates provided by the MercedWRM have a qualitative medium level of accuracy.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimates were generated by the Merced National Wildlife Refuge based on daily estimates for several subunits of their management area, developed internally by Refuge staff.
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The estimates provided by the Merced National Wildlife Refuge have a qualitative medium level of accuracy.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: This estimate is from Municipal Wells provided by the Municipal purveyers in the basin.

LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimated groundwater pumping by Ag lands by each GSA area is the residual of = crop evapotranspiration - groundwater pumping - SW diversions reported - SW estimated riparian diversions - applied recycled water
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Actual crop evapotranspiration was calculated by ITRC using METRIC approach and is estimated to have an accuracy of 90 percent. The accuracy of the total water supply (metered) was about 10 percent of the total demand was assumed to be supplied with riparian water rights.  The accuracy was further reduced by elminating the demand between August through October, during the harvest season, resulting in the estimated groundwater pumping being reduced by 50 percent.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater pumped by small community water systems and industrial users could not be located.  Population was available for some systems from Drinking Water Watch.  Used assumed 0.25 acre-feet per person to estimate.  Otherwise assumed total annual production of 100 AF.  Could not locate groundwater pumping for remedial activities.  Used previous year production totals.
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The accuracy is likely about 50%.  Water use per person was based on statewide average of family of four using 1 acre foot per year.  Unknown number of people in some cases and if industrial activity required water.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION:
Meter data includes: 
(1) Groundwater wells operated by the City of Chowchilla for urban water use. Potable water demand in the City of Chowchilla is currently met exclusively through groundwater, which is pumped directly into the distribution system to meet demands in the Urban Water Use Sector. The remaining rural residential and domestic water demand is estimated based on an urban lands water balance.
(2) Groundwater wells operated by certain landowners in TTWD GSA who are parties to the Subsidence Control Measures Agreement. A portion of agricultural water demand in areas managed by those landowners is met through groundwater pumping, and is metered and reported under the provisions of the Subsidence Control Measures Agreement.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated based on the typical field accuracy of meters, and the combined accuracy of volumes in the City of Chowchilla system (accounting for metered groundwater, metered deliveries, unmetered deliveries, and apparent losses).
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Calculated as the closure of a Land Surface System water budget for the Chowchilla Subbasin (closure of each water use sector was calculated separately for each subregion, accounting for measured pumping). All other Land Surface System water budget components were quantified using measured volumes (where available) or using unitized IWFM Demand Calculator (IDC) model results multiplied by land use areas. Land use data was developed from Land IQ data, crop reports, and County Agriculture Commission land use data.
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Typical uncertainty when calculated for Land Surface System water balance closure. Uncertainty of the water budget closure is calculated as the combined uncertainty of all other water budget flow paths following the procedure described by Clemmens and Burt (1997), as discussed in the Annual Report. The uncertainties of all other flow paths are quantified based on measurement device accuracy, technical literature, or professional judgment.

MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: unknown
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Agricultural extractions were estimated from land use and climate data using the Irrigation Demand Calculator (IDC) within Cosumnes, South American, and North American Model (CoSANA). Domestic (Agricultural-Residential [Ag-Res]) extractions were estimated based on representative indoor and outdoor water use and estimated number of residential parcels in the Basin.
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Land use accuracy of 95% based on ground-truthing efforts and comparisons with other mapping methods (e.g., remote sensing).

OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: unknown

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Meters and comparable measuring devices operated by local water agencies, water purveyors and city water departments in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: These data were collected from well meters by experienced water agency staff following monitoring protocols for urban and agricultural water supply.  Meters are considered to be well-maintained and tested; therefore, reported data meet high accuracy levels in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards. In accordance with these standards for water supply, meter accuracy is assumed to range between 0% and 5%.
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: IWFM Demand Calculator (IDC) in C2VSimFG-Kern
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated groundwater extractions is based on simulation results of the IDC tool within C2VSimFG-Kern model.  The water balance accuracy of the groundwater model is considered to be medium, approximately ranging between 10% and 20%.  Input data based on metered data is assumed to have an accuracy of 0% to 5%.  The agricultural pumping, which is the largest component of groundwater extraction, are estimated using the ITRC-METRIC based ET Rates.  ITRC (2020) lists the accuracy of the ET measurements as ranging from 7% to 10%. Land use is based on Kern County Agricultural Commissioner annual land use data including crop type.  Soil properties are assumed are based on local soil survey data which have a higher level of uncertainty.  Applying a weighted average of these inputs to the overall water budget produces a relative accuracy for the land use calculation for agricultural groundwater extractions ranging between 10% and 20%.

LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Based on accuracy of Crop evapotranspiration estimates

OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Accuracy based on professional judgement

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extraction data from groundwater agencies located in the Merced Subbasin were directly measured. Data are a mixture of metered and some data from pump tests using run time data.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Metered data have a qualitative high level of accuracy. Many agencies reported metered groundwater pumping data and did not have information on an exact percent accuracy.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extractions from private irrigators and domestic wells are estimated by the Merced Water Resources Model (MercedWRM) based on factors including land use, evapotranspiration, and population. Details about the MercedWRM can be found in the GSP and Section 3.5.2 of the Annual Report.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The estimates provided by the MercedWRM have a qualitative medium level of accuracy.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimates were generated by the Merced National Wildlife Refuge based on daily estimates for several subunits of their management area, developed internally by Refuge staff.
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The estimates provided by the Merced National Wildlife Refuge have a qualitative medium level of accuracy.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: This estimate is from Municipal Wells provided by the Municipal purveyers in the basin.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Model accuracy was not quatified and a "high" level of accuracy is assumed.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: The IWFM model, C2VSimTM was used to estimate private agricultural groundwater production through the IDC which uses a combination of data on land use, cropping mix, precipitation, evapotranspiration, soil moiusture, and respective parameters.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Model accuracy was not quantified and a "medium" level of accuracy is assumed.

MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Manufacturers Testing

OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: General Urban Water Use.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Monthly readings
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Provided by meter manufacturer
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: For Ag lands, actual crop evapotraspiration (ETc) was calculated OpenET and eeMETRIC approach where ETc - Surface Water diversions or recycled water by each GSA area was >0, value reported as groundwater pumping.  Oct thru Jan not reported as these months include harvest season and months during which the crops were not planted or irrigated (See Appendix C for details). Not reported for March and April due to rain and carry-over effects.
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Actual crop evapotranspiration was calculated using OpenET and eeMETRIC approach and is estimated to have an accuracy of 90 percent. Total demand included an estimate of about 6,000 AF of water that was assumed to be supplied with riparian water rights.  Surface water diversions were not availabe for WY2023 so 2017 diverisions from SWRCB were used since it was a similar wet year.  The accuracy was further reduced by elminating the demand between August through October, during the harvest season.  The results were further refined to remove months where precipitation would have prevented growers from planting or carry over from previous month's rain events.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Total data available from meters
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Ag Pumping - Step 1: Determine Cropping Pattern; Step 2: Determine total crop demand (Total Crop Demand = Annual Crop Acreage * Annual Crop ETc); Step 3: Adjust for effective precipitation and irrigation efficiency [Farm Demand = (Total Crop Demand - Effective Precipitation) / Irrigation Efficiency]; Step 4: Calculate Un-met Demand (Un-met Demand = Farm Demand - Surface Water Deliveries)
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Crop ET estimated from model. Outside of Agricultural lands estimated with best available data.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Other - Step 1: Find rural population for current year; Step 2: Multiply Rural Pop by 179 gal per capita (Assumed value from Public Policy Institute of California's Water and the Future of the San Joaquin Valley February 2019 )
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Population pulled from Census Bureau; Per capita volume uses estimate made from 2017 drought period

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Metered data provided by WRMWSD, AEWSD, and compiled from the State Board Electronic Annual Report System.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: reported data are assumed to have a high level of accuracy, with a precision of 0.01 AF or 0.001 AF
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Agricultural pumping is estimated by the Basin’s Soil Moisture Budget (SMB) Accounting model and input into the White Wolf Groundwater Flow Model (WWGFM) domain. Evaporation from shallow groundwater is simulated by the WWGFM, based on a 7-ft extinction depth and historical pan evaporation rates.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extractions estimated by the SMB have a medium degree of accuracy, ranging from 10% to 20% with a precision of 100 AF to 1,000 AF. Evaporation from shallow groundwater estimated by the WWGFM has a medium degree of accuracy, ranging from 40% to 50%.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: State Board Electronic Annual Report data Jan 2023 - Sept 2023 unavailable, and reflects repeat values from Jan 2022 - Sept 2022.
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: reported data are assumed to have a high level of accuracy, with a precision of 0.01 AF; however given 2022 was a critically dry year and 2023 was a wet year, estimates are only 50% accurate.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION:
Meter data includes: 
(1) Groundwater wells operated by the City of Chowchilla for urban water use. Potable water demand in the City of Chowchilla is currently met exclusively through groundwater, which is pumped directly into the distribution system to meet demands in the Urban Water Use Sector.
(2) Groundwater wells operated by certain landowners in TTWD GSA who are parties to the Subsidence Control Measures Agreement. A portion of agricultural water demand in areas managed by those landowners is met through groundwater pumping, and is metered and reported under the provisions of the Subsidence Control Measures Agreement.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated based on the typical field accuracy of meters, and the combined accuracy of volumes in the City of Chowchilla system (accounting for metered groundwater, metered deliveries, unmetered deliveries, and apparent losses).
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimated using an integrated hydrologic model (MCSim) based on land use, ET, available surface water supplies, and other available data. Volume is rounded to match rounded total groundwater extraction reported in Part A. It is noted that this is a refinement of the approach in prior Annual Reports.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Typical uncertainty of groundwater extraction estimates from an integrated hydrologic model approach, considering the combined uncertainty of data sources and other flow paths.

MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: unknown
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Agricultural extractions were estimated from land use and climate data using the Irrigation Demand Cacculator within the Cosumnes, South American, and North American (CoSANA) model. Domestic (i.e. Ag-Res) extractions were estimated based on representative indoor and outdoor water use and the estimated number of residential parcels in the Basin.
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Land use accuracy of 95% based on ground-truthing efforts and comparisons with other mapping methods (e.g., remote sensing).

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Meters and comparable measuring devices operated by local water agencies, water purveyors and city water departments in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: These data were collected from well meters by experienced water agency staff following monitoring protocols for urban and agricultural water supply.  Meters are considered to be well-maintained and tested; therefore, reported data meet high accuracy levels in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards. In accordance with these standards for water supply, meter accuracy is assumed to range between 0% and 5%.
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: IWFM Demand Calculator (IDC) in C2VSimFG-Kern
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated groundwater extractions is based on simulation results of the IDC tool within C2VSimFG-Kern model.  The water balance accuracy of the groundwater model is considered to be medium, approximately ranging between 10% and 20%.  Input data based on metered data is assumed to have an accuracy of 0% to 5%.  The agricultural pumping, which is the largest component of groundwater extraction, are estimated using the ITRC-METRIC based ET Rates.  ITRC (2020) lists the accuracy of the ET measurements as ranging from 7% to 10%. Land use is based on Kern County Agricultural Commissioner annual land use data including crop type.  Soil properties are assumed are based on local soil survey data which have a higher level of uncertainty.  Applying a weighted average of these inputs to the overall water budget produces a relative accuracy for the land use calculation for agricultural groundwater extractions ranging between 10% and 20%.

LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Calculated pumping from crop water demands minus surface water deliveries

OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Rural domestic pumping estimated based on vegetated area and standard indoor use
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Accuracy based on professional judgement

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION:
Groundwater wells operated by the City of Madera for urban water use are 100% metered. The City's potable water supplies are exclusively from groundwater, which is pumped directly into the distribution system to meet demands in the Urban Water Use Sector (City of Madera GSA).
Madera Water District operates agricultural groundwater wells that are also 100% metered. Groundwater pumped from these wells serve demands in the Agricultural Water Use Sector (Madera Water District GSA).
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated based on the typical field accuracy of meters, and the combined accuracy of volumes in distribution systems (accounting for metered groundwater, metered deliveries, unmetered deliveries, and apparent losses)
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimated using an integrated hydrologic model (MCSim) based on land use, ET, available surface water supplies, and other available data. Volume is rounded to match rounded total groundwater extraction reported in Part A. It is noted that this is a refinement of the approach in prior Annual Reports; this refinement was made as part of efforts to bring consistency across the Subbasin water budget in the coordinated Subbasin Annual Report document.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Typical uncertainty of groundwater extraction estimates from an integrated hydrologic model approach, considering the combined uncertainty of data sources and other flow paths.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Meters and comparable measuring devices operated by local water agencies, water purveyors and city water departments in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: These data were collected from well meters by experienced water agency staff following monitoring protocols for urban and agricultural water supply.  Meters are considered to be well-maintained and tested; therefore, reported data meet high accuracy levels in compliance with AWWA and other relevant standards. In accordance with these standards for water supply, meter accuracy is assumed to range between 0% and 5%.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: IWFM Demand Calculator (IDC) in Kern-IWFM
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Calibration

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Flow meters

LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Calculated pumping from crop water demands minus surface water deliveries

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION:
Madera Water District operates agricultural groundwater wells that are 100% metered. Groundwater pumped from these wells serve demands in the Agricultural Water Use Sector (Madera Water District GSA).
Groundwater wells operated by the City of Madera for urban water use are 100% metered. The City's potable water supplies are exclusively from groundwater, which is pumped directly into the distribution system to meet demands in the Urban Water Use Sector (City of Madera GSA).
Root Creek Water District provided measured groundwater pumping data for all urban groundwater extraction in the Urban Water Use Sector (Root Creek Water District GSA).
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated based on the typical field accuracy of meters, and the typical combined accuracy of volumes in urban distribution systems (accounting for metered groundwater, metered deliveries, unmetered deliveries, and apparent losses)
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimated using an integrated hydrologic model (MCSim) based on land use, ET, available surface water supplies, and other available data. Volume is rounded to match rounded total groundwater extraction reported in Part A. It is noted that this is a refinement of the approach in Annual Reports prior to WY2024. This refinement was made as part of efforts to bring consistency across the Subbasin water budget in the coordinated Subbasin Annual Report document.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Typical uncertainty of groundwater extraction estimates from an integrated hydrologic model approach, considering the combined uncertainty of data sources and other flow paths.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extraction data from groundwater agencies located in the Merced Subbasin were directly measured. Data are a mixture of metered and some data from pump tests using run time data.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Metered data have a qualitative high level of accuracy. Many agencies reported metered groundwater pumping data and did not have information on an exact percent accuracy.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extractions from private irrigators and domestic wells are estimated by the Merced Water Resources Model (MercedWRM) based on factors including land use, evapotranspiration, and population. Details about the MercedWRM can be found in the GSP and Section 3.4.2 of the Annual Report.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The estimates provided by the MercedWRM have a qualitative medium level of accuracy.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimates were generated by the Merced National Wildlife Refuge based on daily estimates for several subunits of their management area, developed internally by Refuge staff.
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: The estimates provided by the Merced National Wildlife Refuge have a qualitative medium level of accuracy.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: This estimate is from Municipal Wells provided by the Municipal purveyers in the basin.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Model accuracy was not quatified and a "high" level of accuracy is assumed.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: The IWFM model, C2VSimTM was used to estimate private agricultural groundwater production through the IDC which uses a combination of data on land use, cropping mix, precipitation, evapotranspiration, soil moiusture, and respective parameters.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Model accuracy was not quatified and a "medium" level of accuracy is assumed.

MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Estimated
LUSE_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Ag Pumping - Step 1: Determine Cropping Pattern; Step 2: Determine total crop demand (Total Crop Demand = Annual Crop Acreage * Annual Crop ETc); Step 3: Adjust for effective precipitation and irrigation efficiency [Farm Demand = (Total Crop Demand - Effective Precipitation) / Irrigation Efficiency]; Step 4: Calculate Un-met Demand (Un-met Demand = Farm Demand - Surface Water Deliveries)
LUSE_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Crop ET estimated from model. Outside of Agricultural lands estimated with best available data.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Other - Step 1: Find rural population for current year; Step 2: Multiply Rural Pop by 179 gal per capita (Assumed value from Public Policy Institute of California's Water and the Future of the San Joaquin Valley February 2019 )
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Population pulled from Census Bureau; Per capita volume uses estimate made from 2017 drought period

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Agricultural production wells and municipal wells for urban use
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: +/-5% accuracy assumed for meters
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Back calculated from remotely sensed satellite ETc data and irrigation efficiency estimates based on CDWR land use maps or field surveys
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: ETc data been proven to have approximately 90% accurracy based on pilot studies performed within the Tule Subbasin.  Irrigation efficiencies are subject to error being they are derived from outdated CDWR land use data and correlated to common irrigation methods for any given crop.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: This estimate is from Municipal Wells provided by the Municipal purveyors in the basin.

ERECORDS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Electrical records accuracy was not quantified, and a "high" level of accuracy is assumed.
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: The IWFM model, C2VSimTM was used to estimate private groundwater production. Simulated groundwater production is based on published data, including land use, precipitation, evapotranspiration, population, per-capita water use, and soil parameters.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Model accuracy was not quantified, and a "medium" level of accuracy is assumed.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: 979 metered irrigation wells
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Accuracy can vary between meter models and calibration. Accuracy of 2% is the reported meter accuracy.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Estimate based on number of domestic wells and PWS self-reported values extrapolated to current WY
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Percent was calculated based on the number of domestic wells and PWS self-reported values.

MTRS_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Metered data provided by WRMWSD, AEWSD, and compiled from the SAFER Clearinghouse.
MTRS_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: reported data are assumed to have a high level of accuracy, with a precision of 0.01 AF or 0.001 AF
GW_MODEL_METHOD_EXPLANATION: Agricultural pumping and native vegetation use are estimated by the Basin’s Soil Moisture Budget (SMB) Accounting model and input into the White Wolf Groundwater Flow Model (WWGFM) domain. Evaporation from shallow groundwater is simulated by the WWGFM, based on a 7-ft extinction depth and historical pan evaporation rates.
GW_MODEL_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Groundwater extractions estimated by the SMB have a medium degree of accuracy, ranging from 10% to 20% with a precision of 100 AF to 1,000 AF. Evaporation from shallow groundwater estimated by the WWGFM has a medium degree of accuracy, ranging from 40% to 50%.
OTHER_METHOD_EXPLANATION: There are approximately 18 to 20 households within the Basin supplied by unmetered domestic wells. Local residential (single family and multi-family) water use is approximately 84 gallons per capita per day (Arvin Community Services District, 2021). The 2024 American Community Survey estimates that the average household size in the Mettler Census Designated Place is 3.11 people (U.S. Census Bureau, n.d.).
OTHER_ACCURACY_EXPLANATION: Based on average estimated values
